subject: Social Studies
description: Examine how the development of Oklahoma's major economic activities have contributed to the growth of the state, including, mining and energy industry, agriculture, aviation, tourism, tribal enterprises, and military installations.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how Oklahoma's main industries like mining, farming, aviation, tourism, tribal businesses, and the military have helped the state grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize how the factors of scarcity and surplus and the laws of supply and demand of natural and human resources require people to make choices about producing and consuming goods and services.
description_plain: Your child understands that when things are scarce or plentiful, people have to make choices about what to make and use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare differences among human, natural, and capital resources used to produce goods and services.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between human resources (people), natural resources (like water or trees), and capital resources (tools and machines) used to make goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will identify and describe basic economic activities creating prosperity in the state of Oklahoma.
description_plain: Your child will identify and describe the main economic activities that help Oklahoma prosper.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine notable historic and present-day Oklahomans utilizing biographies and information texts such as Jim Thorpe, Sequoyah, Will Rogers, Wiley Post, Mickey Mantle, Shannon Lucid, Bill Pickett, Clara Luper, and Maria Tallchief.
description_plain: Your child can learn about important Oklahomans from the past and present, like Jim Thorpe and Will Rogers, by reading biographies and information books.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how Oklahomans come together to help one another during difficult times, such as recovering from the bombing of the Oklahoma City Murrah Building, exhibiting what has become the "Oklahoma Standard".
description_plain: Your child understands how people in Oklahoma help each other during hard times, like after the Oklahoma City bombing, showing the 'Oklahoma Standard.'

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the contributions of Oklahoma's military personnel, including the Buffalo Soldiers, the code talkers, and the 45th Infantry.
description_plain: Your child can describe how Oklahoma's military groups, like the Buffalo Soldiers and code talkers, have contributed to history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Commemorate Statehood Day, November 16, as the joining of Indian and Oklahoma Territories.
description_plain: Your child will remember that Statehood Day on November 16 marks when Indian and Oklahoma Territories joined to become a state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between the points of view of both American Indians and settlers regarding the opening of territories in Oklahoma for settlement.
description_plain: Your child can explain the different views of American Indians and settlers about opening Oklahoma lands for settlement.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe cowboy life and cattle drives as typified by experiences along such routes as the Chisholm Trail and the impact of Mexican ranching traditions on the cattle industry and cowboy culture.
description_plain: Your child can describe what cowboy life and cattle drives were like, including how Mexican ranching traditions influenced them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the migrations, settlements, relocations and forced removals of American Indians.
description_plain: Your child understands the movements and forced removals of American Indians in Oklahoma's history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe early expeditions into Oklahoma such as those of Coronado, Washington Irving, and George Catlin.
description_plain: Your child can describe early explorers who traveled through Oklahoma, like Coronado and Washington Irving.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify cultural similarities and differences of the existing sovereign tribal nations in Oklahoma, especially those near the local community.
description_plain: Your child can identify how different tribal nations in Oklahoma are alike and different, especially those near your community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe American Indian pre-contact cultures that have inhabited what is now Oklahoma, such as the Spiro Mound Builders.
description_plain: Your child can describe the cultures of American Indians who lived in Oklahoma before Europeans arrived, like the Spiro Mound Builders.

subject: Social Studies
description: Read and interpret primary sources related to key events in Oklahoma's past.
description_plain: Your child can read and understand original documents about important events in Oklahoma's history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand and describe the relationship between historic events and chronology through the creation of basic timelines.
description_plain: Your child can put historic events in order by making simple timelines.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will analyze the significant events and historic personalities contributing to the development of the state of Oklahoma.
description_plain: Your child will learn about important events and people who helped shape Oklahoma.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of renewable and non-renewable resources and evaluate the role of citizens in conserving natural resources.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between resources that can be replaced and those that cannot, and why people should help save natural resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize how Oklahomans affect and change their environments such as the construction of the McClellan-Kerr Arkansas River Navigation System, creation of recreational lakes by the building of dams, irrigation of croplands, and the establishment of wildlife refuges.
description_plain: Your child understands how people in Oklahoma have changed their environment by building things like dams, lakes, and wildlife areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize how the weather and the environment have impacted the economy of Oklahoma in events such as the Dust Bowl, floods, and tornadoes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how weather events like dust storms, floods, and tornadoes have affected Oklahoma's economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how pioneers to Oklahoma adapted to and modified their environment, such as sod houses, windmills, and crops.
description_plain: Your child can describe how early settlers in Oklahoma changed their environment by building sod houses, using windmills, and planting crops.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how early American Indians used Oklahoma's natural resources, such as bison hunting, fur trading, and farming.
description_plain: Your child understands how early American Indians used Oklahoma's natural resources for hunting, trading, and farming.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the interaction of the environment and the peoples of Oklahoma.
description_plain: Your child will learn how people and the environment in Oklahoma affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the six states bordering Oklahoma on a map.
description_plain: Your child can find and name the six states that border Oklahoma on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the climate and various natural vegetation zones found in Oklahoma.
description_plain: Your child can describe Oklahoma's climate and the different types of plants that grow in the state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify Oklahoma's major metropolitan centers and cities on a political map.
description_plain: Your child can find Oklahoma's main cities on a political map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify Oklahoma's major landforms and bodies of water on a physical map.
description_plain: Your child can find Oklahoma's major landforms and bodies of water on a physical map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret thematic maps of Oklahoma with the essential map elements of title, legend, scale, and directional indicators.
description_plain: Your child can read Oklahoma maps by using the title, legend, scale, and directions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the state of Oklahoma using relative location, absolute location (latitude and longitude), direction, scale, size, and shape using physical and political maps.
description_plain: Your child can locate Oklahoma on maps using directions, size, shape, and coordinates like latitude and longitude.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine Oklahoma's political and physical features.
description_plain: Your child will learn about Oklahoma's political and physical features.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will examine Oklahoma's geography and how people of Oklahoma interact with their environment.
description_plain: Your child will study Oklahoma's geography and how people live and work with the land.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define the concept of civic virtue and responsibilities of the citizen at the local, state, and tribal levels, including respect for diversity.
description_plain: Your child can explain what it means to be a good citizen and their responsibilities in local, state, and tribal communities, including respecting different cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe relationships between people and events of the past, including those commemorated on national, state, and community holidays.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people and events from the past are connected and remembered through holidays.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the historical significance of the symbols of Oklahoma including the Oklahoma State Seal and the Oklahoma Flag; explain how the name of Oklahoma is derived from the Choctaw language.
description_plain: Your child can explain the meaning of Oklahoma's symbols like the state seal and flag, and know that the state's name comes from the Choctaw language.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that tribal governments in Oklahoma have a right to self-government known as sovereignty.
description_plain: Your child understands that tribal governments in Oklahoma have the right to govern themselves.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine and determine the main purposes of Oklahoma's state government and identify elected leaders of the state of Oklahoma and the three branches of government.
description_plain: Your child can explain what Oklahoma's state government does, name its elected leaders, and identify the three branches of government.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will analyze the traits of good citizens.
description_plain: Your child understands what makes a good citizen by learning about their traits.

subject: Social Studies
description: engage in authentic inquiry to acquire, refine, and share knowledge through written presentations related to social studies.
description_plain: Your child can research social studies topics and share what they learn through writing.

subject: Social Studies
description: summarize and paraphrase, integrate evidence, and cite sources to create written products, research projects, and presentations for multiple purposes related to social studies content.
description_plain: Your child can summarize information, use evidence, and give credit to sources when writing reports and presentations about social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will apply effective communication skills by developing a variety of evidence-based written products designed for multiple purposes and tasks, in order to demonstrate their understandings of social studies concepts, ideas, and content.
description_plain: Your child uses good communication skills to write different kinds of papers that show what they understand about social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: apply critical reading and thinking skills to interpret, evaluate, and respond to a variety of complex texts from historical, ethnic, and global perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can read and think carefully about complex social studies texts from different cultures and viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: comprehend, evaluate, and synthesize textual sources to acquire and refine knowledge in the social studies.
description_plain: Your child can understand, judge, and combine information from social studies texts to learn more.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will engage in critical, active reading of grade-level appropriate primary and secondary sources related to key social studies concepts, including frequent analysis and interpretation of informational sources.
description_plain: Your child reads and analyzes social studies materials to understand important ideas and information.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyze the principles of economic systems and develop an understanding of the benefits of a market system in local, national, and global settings.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different economic systems work and why market systems are helpful locally and worldwide.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate an understanding of geographic concepts and develop mastery of geographic tools and ways of thinking in order to become geographically informed.
description_plain: Your child understands geography and uses tools like maps to learn about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: develop skills and practices which demonstrate an understanding that historical inquiry is based on the analysis and evaluation of evidence and its credibility.
description_plain: Your child learns how historians study and check evidence to understand the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will utilize interdisciplinary tools and master the basic concepts of the social studies in order to acquire and apply content understanding in all related fields of study.
description_plain: Your child uses skills from different subjects to understand social studies topics better.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate the ability to investigate problems taking into consideration multiple points of view represented in arguments, structure of an explanation, and other sources.
description_plain: Your child can look at problems from different viewpoints and understand how arguments are made.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate the capability for developing essential, compelling, and supporting questions that address authentic civic issues.
description_plain: Your child can create important questions about real civic issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will determine the kinds of sources that will be helpful in answering essential, compelling, and supporting questions addressing authentic civic issues.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find the best sources to answer important questions about civic topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate understanding of the processes and rules by which groups of people make decisions, govern themselves, and address public problems.
description_plain: Your child understands how groups make decisions, govern themselves, and solve public problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate an understanding of the important institutions of their society and the principles that these institutions are intended to reflect.
description_plain: Your child knows about important institutions in society and the ideas they represent.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate an understanding of the virtues that citizens should use when interacting with each other and the virtues that guide official government institutions.
description_plain: Your child understands the good qualities citizens and government officials should have when working together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will understand the principles of government, the benefits of democratic systems, and their responsibilities as citizens.
description_plain: Your child understands how government works, why democracy is good, and what it means to be a responsible citizen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of changes in land use in local communities and how the physical environment can be stressed by human activities.
description_plain: Your child can find examples of how land use has changed in local communities and how people’s actions can harm the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways in which humans can change ecosystems, such as clearing forests, draining wetlands, and diverting waterways, by examining present-day issues related to the use of resources.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people change ecosystems by things like cutting down forests, draining wetlands, and changing waterways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how economic activities can threaten the physical environment.
description_plain: Your child understands how economic activities can sometimes harm the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the relative location of natural resources, such as fossil fuels, minerals and soils, and their relationship to each region's major economic activities, including agriculture, manufacturing, transportation, energy, and services.
description_plain: Your child can describe where natural resources like fossil fuels, minerals, and soil are found and how they relate to each region’s main jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major economic activities of each region of the United States by comparing how people satisfy their basic needs through the production of goods and services.
description_plain: Your child can identify the main jobs people do in different parts of the United States to meet their basic needs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate on a map the major cities of the United States, including their relative location to natural resources and transportation routes.
description_plain: Your child can find major U.S. cities on a map and explain how they are located near natural resources and transportation routes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the patterns and networks of economic interdependence among regions of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands how different regions in the U.S. depend on each other for goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how physical environments can provide both opportunities and limitations for human activity.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the physical environment can help or limit what people do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between renewable and nonrenewable resources.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between resources that can be replaced and those that cannot.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans depend upon the physical environment for food, shelter, and economic activities.
description_plain: Your child understands how people rely on the environment for food, shelter, and jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how humans adapt to and modify their environments in order to survive and grow.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people change and adjust to their environment to live and grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will identify basic economic activities of the United States.
description_plain: Your child will learn about the main types of economic activities in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics of culture affect the ways in which people live.
description_plain: Your child can explain how culture influences the way people live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of culture (language, customs, beliefs, food, clothing, shelter) and compare the cultural characteristics of different regions of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can identify parts of culture like language, customs, beliefs, food, clothing, and shelter, and compare these in different U.S. regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the characteristics of culture, including the distribution and complexity of the regions of the United States.
description_plain: Your child will learn about culture and how it varies across different parts of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the impact of the Columbian Exchange on American Indian groups, African slaves and European settlers, including agriculture, trade, culture, military alliances, control of territory, and the sudden and significant decline of indigenous peoples.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Columbian Exchange affected American Indians, African slaves, and European settlers in farming, trade, culture, and population.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify push and pull factors of human migration.
description_plain: Your child can identify reasons why people move from one place to another.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the reasons for key expeditions of North America by Spain, France, and England and their impact on the development of each region.
description_plain: Your child can summarize why Spain, France, and England explored North America and how their trips affected each region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major American Indian groups and their ways of life in each region, including economic activities, customs, and viewpoints on land usage and ownership.
description_plain: Your child can name major American Indian groups and describe their ways of life, including their jobs, customs, and ideas about land.

subject: Social Studies
description: Draw conclusions from maps to show how climate, vegetation, natural resources, and historic events affect the location and growth of settlements.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to explain how climate, plants, resources, and history affect where people settle and how towns grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe early settlement patterns of regions in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can identify and describe early settlement patterns in different U.S. regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will analyze the human characteristics of the United States and how geography impacts historic events.
description_plain: Your child will learn how people and geography have shaped the history of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate on a political map the fifty states and the United States capitol.
description_plain: Your child can find all fifty states and the U.S. capital on a political map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how climate and natural processes including floods, wind, and storms impact how we live.
description_plain: Your child can explain how weather and natural events like floods and storms affect how people live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the different climates in the United States using maps, globes, and graphs.
description_plain: Your child can identify and describe different climates in the U.S. using maps and graphs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how physical processes of the Earth's surface impact humans and their environment.
description_plain: Your child understands how Earth's natural processes affect people and their surroundings.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why regions change over time by comparing regions in the past with life in the same regions in the present.
description_plain: Your child can explain how regions change over time by comparing the past and present.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the major physical, cultural, and economic regions of the United States, comparing one's own region to the other regions.
description_plain: Your child can identify major physical, cultural, and economic regions in the U.S. and compare their own region to others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people create regions using common geographic characteristics.
description_plain: Your child understands how people create regions based on shared geographic features.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the location and characteristics of the major ecosystems in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can describe where major ecosystems are in the U.S. and what makes them special.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the physical characteristics of places, including the major landforms, bodies of water, vegetation and climates in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can identify important landforms, bodies of water, plants, and climates in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major physical features in the United States and analyze how physical processes shape places.
description_plain: Your child can find major physical features in the U.S. and explain how natural forces shape them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use latitude and longitude to identify the location of physical and human features of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can use latitude and longitude to find places and features in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret aerial photographs, satellite images and thematic maps to locate and identify physical and human features of the United States and North America.
description_plain: Your child can read aerial photos, satellite images, and special maps to find physical and human features in the U.S. and North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use and describe various elements of maps, including keys/legends, scale, cardinal, and intermediate directions.
description_plain: Your child can use map parts like keys, scale, and directions to understand maps better.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps and other geographic representations (such as globes and graphs), tools, and technologies to acquire, process, and report information from a spatial perspective.
description_plain: Your child can use maps, globes, and other tools to find and share information about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will examine the physical geography and environments of the United States.
description_plain: Your child will learn about the physical geography and environments of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify present-day examples to conserve natural resources and the development of alternative, sustainable energy sources.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how people save natural resources and use alternative energy sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the benefits of participation in recycling and anti-littering activities.
description_plain: Your child can explain why recycling and not littering help keep the environment clean and healthy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the role of citizens as responsible stewards of natural resources and the environment.
description_plain: Your child understands that citizens take care of natural resources and the environment by being responsible.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare powers exercised by the local, state, and national levels of governments, recognizing tribal sovereignty as a tribal nation's inherent right to self-govern.
description_plain: Your child can tell how local, state, and national governments share different powers and knows that tribal nations govern themselves.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the necessity of respect for diversity of the individual and diversity of groups comprising American society.
description_plain: Your child understands why it’s important to respect people from different backgrounds and groups in America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the concept of civic responsibilities, including respect for the law, the necessity for compromise, civic participation, and public service.
description_plain: Your child can explain what it means to be a good citizen, like following laws, working together, voting, and helping the community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the concepts of democracy and representative government, including the rule of law, equality, the common good, and individual rights.
description_plain: Your child understands basic ideas about democracy and how government represents people, follows laws, treats everyone fairly, and protects rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will describe the features of self-government and the role of citizens of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the United States government works and what citizens do to help.

description: engage in authentic inquiry to acquire, refine, and share knowledge through written presentations related to social studies.
description_plain: Your child can research social studies topics and share what they learn through writing.

description_plain: Your child can read and think carefully about complex social studies texts from different cultures and viewpoints.

description: registration for military service
description_plain: Your child knows about registering for military service as a responsibility of citizens.

description: obedience to laws

description: payment of taxes

description: service on trial juries
description_plain: service on trial juries

description: engagement in civil discourse
description_plain: engagement in civil discourse

subject: Social Studies
description: becoming informed voters
description_plain: becoming informed voters

subject: Social Studies
description: registration and voting in public elections
description_plain: Your child understands how citizens register and vote in public elections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the responsibilities of United States citizens including:
description_plain: Your child can describe important duties of U.S. citizens, like voting and following laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the roles of Congress, the President, and the Supreme Court in the legislative process.
description_plain: Your child can explain what Congress, the President, and the Supreme Court do to make and apply laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: shared powers between the federal and state governments.
description_plain: Your child understands that federal and state governments share some powers.

subject: Social Studies
description: the system of checks and balances
description_plain: Your child knows how the government’s branches check each other to keep power balanced.

subject: Social Studies
description: separation of powers among three branches of government
description_plain: Your child can name the three branches of government and explain what each one does.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the key principles of government established in the Constitution of the United States including:
description_plain: Your child understands important ideas from the U.S. Constitution about how government works.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will describe the structure and responsibilities of the American system of government and the role of the individual citizen.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the American government is set up and what citizens’ roles are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the Constitution of the United States was amended to include the Bill of Rights and summarize the liberties protected in each of the ten amendments.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Bill of Rights was added to the Constitution and what freedoms it protects.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the viewpoints of the Federalists, led by James Madison, and Anti-Federalists, such as George Mason, over the addition of a bill of rights.
description_plain: Your child understands the different opinions people had about adding the Bill of Rights to the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the relationship between the federal government and sovereign American Indian nations, as established under the Constitution of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the federal government works with American Indian nations as separate governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the purposes and basic responsibilities of government as described in the Preamble of the Constitution of the United States, which established the supreme law of the land.
description_plain: Your child understands the main goals and duties of government from the Constitution’s introduction.

subject: Social Studies
description: President of the Convention, George Washington
description_plain: Your child knows that George Washington was the leader of the group that wrote the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Father of the Constitution, James Madison
description_plain: Your child understands that James Madison is called the Father of the Constitution for his important role.

subject: Social Studies
description: Three-fifths Compromise and its maintenance of the institution of slavery
description_plain: Your child can explain the Three-fifths Compromise and how it kept slavery in place.

subject: Social Studies
description: Great Compromise
description_plain: Great Compromise

subject: Social Studies
description: Virginia and New Jersey Plans
description_plain: Virginia and New Jersey Plans

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key leaders and explain the debates and compromises of the Constitutional Convention, including:
description_plain: Your child can name key leaders and explain the important debates and agreements made when writing the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate issues and events that led to the Constitutional Convention, including a weak national government and Shays' Rebellion.
description_plain: Your child understands problems like a weak government and Shays' Rebellion that led to the Constitutional Convention.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will examine the formation of the American system of government following the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child will learn how the American government was created after the Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the contributions of key individuals involved in the American Revolution including Patrick Henry, Samuel Adams, John Adams, Abigail Adams, Paul Revere, Nathan Hale, John Paul Jones, Thayendanegea (Joseph Brant), Nancy Ward the Beloved Woman of the Cherokee, Marquis de Lafayette, Benjamin Franklin, Mercy Otis Warren, and Phillis Wheatley.
description_plain: Your child can name important people who helped during the American Revolution and what they did.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the points of view of major groups that remained loyal to Britain, joined the patriot cause, or remained neutral.
description_plain: Your child understands the different sides people took during the Revolution: loyal to Britain, patriots, or neutral.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the relationships of significant military and diplomatic events of the Revolutionary War including the leadership of General George Washington, experiences of Valley Forge, impact of the battles of Bunker Hill, Trenton, Saratoga, Yorktown, and the Treaty of Paris in 1783.
description_plain: Your child can explain key battles and events during the Revolutionary War and how leaders like George Washington helped.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the advantages and disadvantages of the British and the American colonies at the eve and during the Revolutionary War, including political and military leadership, military strength, population, resources, foreign alliances, and motivations for fighting.
description_plain: Your child can compare the strengths and weaknesses of the British and American sides during the Revolutionary War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the Iroquois Confederacy's representative government to the early attempts of the colonies to unite as one nation.
description_plain: Your child understands how the Iroquois Confederacy’s government was like early efforts to unite the colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of the Articles of Confederation as the first American national system of government under which the colonies waged a war in order to gain independence.
description_plain: Your child knows that the Articles of Confederation was the first government for the colonies during the fight for independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: explain specific colonial grievances
description_plain: Your child can explain specific complaints the colonists had against British rule.

subject: Social Studies
description: establish the principle of self-government and consent of the governed
description_plain: Your child understands that people have the right to govern themselves and agree on their leaders.

subject: Social Studies
description: define the purpose of government
description_plain: Your child can explain why governments exist and what they are supposed to do.

subject: Social Studies
description: declare the equality of all individuals
description_plain: Your child understands that all people are equal.

subject: Social Studies
description: identify natural, unalienable rights, such as life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness
description_plain: Your child knows about natural rights like life, freedom, and the chance to be happy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ideals stated in the Declaration of Independence, drafted by Thomas Jefferson and adopted July 4, 1776, used to:
description_plain: Your child can explain the main ideas in the Declaration of Independence about rights and government.

subject: Social Studies
description: publication of Common Sense, by Thomas Paine
description_plain: Your child knows that Thomas Paine wrote Common Sense to encourage independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: British raids on Lexington and Concord
description_plain: Your child understands that British soldiers attacked Lexington and Concord, starting the Revolutionary War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Coercive Acts (Intolerable Acts)
description_plain: Your child will learn about the Coercive Acts, also called the Intolerable Acts, which were laws that punished the American colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Tea Act and The Boston Tea Party
description_plain: Your child will understand the Tea Act and how the Boston Tea Party was a protest against it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Boston Massacre
description_plain: Boston Massacre

subject: Social Studies
description: Quartering Act
description_plain: Quartering Act

subject: Social Studies
description: boycotts of British goods and the efforts of the Committees of Correspondence
description_plain: Your child can explain how colonists boycotted British goods and how groups called the Committees of Correspondence helped organize these efforts.

subject: Social Studies
description: colonial arguments regarding taxation and rightful representation in Parliament
description_plain: Your child will understand the colonists' arguments about being taxed without having representatives in the British Parliament.

subject: Social Studies
description: Townshend Act
description_plain: Townshend Act

subject: Social Studies
description: Sugar and Stamp Acts
description_plain: Sugar and Stamp Acts

subject: Social Studies
description: Proclamation of 1763
description_plain: Proclamation of 1763

subject: Social Studies
description: French and Indian War
description_plain: French and Indian War

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the causes and effects of significant events leading to armed conflict between the thirteen American colonies and Great Britain including:
description_plain: Your child can explore the important events that caused the American colonies and Great Britain to go to war.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will examine the foundations of the American nation established during the Revolutionary Era.
description_plain: Your child will study how the United States began during the Revolutionary Era.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the experiences of both free and enslaved Africans in the British colonies, including resistance efforts by enslaved peoples and attempts to maintain aspects of African culture.
description_plain: Your child can compare what life was like for free and enslaved Africans in the colonies, including how enslaved people resisted and kept their culture alive.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare daily life in the colonies as experienced by different social classes, plantation owners, farmers, merchants, craftsmen, artisans, and women and children.
description_plain: Your child will compare daily life for different groups in the colonies, like plantation owners, farmers, merchants, craftsmen, women, and children.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that tribal sovereignty is a tribal nation's inherent right to self-govern.
description_plain: Your child understands that tribal sovereignty means Native American tribes have the right to govern themselves.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the evolving relationships between American Indians and the British colonists involving territorial claims.
description_plain: Your child can explain how relationships between American Indians and British colonists changed over land claims.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the forms of self-government in the three colonial regions including the role of religion in the establishment of some colonial governments, the Virginia House of Burgesses, and New England town hall meetings.
description_plain: Your child will learn about how the colonies governed themselves, including the role of religion, the Virginia House of Burgesses, and town meetings in New England.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the international economic and cultural interactions resulting from the triangular trade routes, including the forced migration of Africans through the Transatlantic slave trade and experiences of the Middle Passage.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the triangular trade connected different parts of the world, including the forced movement of Africans through the slave trade and the hardships of the Middle Passage.

subject: Social Studies
description: slave labor central to the growth of the economy
description_plain: Your child understands that slave labor was very important to the colonies' economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: a labor system utilizing indentured servants
description_plain: Your child will learn about how indentured servants worked as part of the colonies' labor system.

subject: Social Studies
description: agriculture and exports as affected by climate and natural resources
description_plain: Your child can explain how farming and exports depended on the climate and natural resources in each colonial region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the economic development of the three colonial regions including:
description_plain: Your child will compare how the three colonial regions developed their economies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the contributions of important citizens and groups to the foundation of the colonies including the Puritans and Quakers, Roger Williams, Anne Hutchinson, William Penn, Lord Baltimore, and James Oglethorpe.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important people and groups like the Puritans, Quakers, Roger Williams, Anne Hutchinson, William Penn, Lord Baltimore, and James Oglethorpe helped start the colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will compare the developments of the New England Colonies, the Middle Colonies, and the Southern Colonies.
description_plain: Your child will compare how the New England, Middle, and Southern Colonies grew and changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how American Indian agricultural practices, such as the Three Sisters, contributed to the early survival of the colonists.
description_plain: Your child understands how American Indian farming methods, like planting the Three Sisters crops, helped early colonists survive.

subject: Social Studies
description: leadership of William Bradford
description_plain: Your child will learn about the leadership of William Bradford in the Plymouth colony.

subject: Social Studies
description: contributions of American Indians including Chief Massasoit and Squanto
description_plain: Your child can explain how American Indians like Chief Massasoit and Squanto helped the Plymouth settlers.

subject: Social Studies
description: practice of self-government established by the Mayflower Compact
description_plain: Your child understands how the Mayflower Compact set up self-government for the Plymouth colony.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the early successes and challenges of the Plymouth settlement including:
description_plain: Your child will learn about the early successes and problems faced by the Plymouth settlement.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the religious, economic, and political motivations of immigrants and indentured servants who migrated to Plymouth.
description_plain: Your child can explain why immigrants and indentured servants came to Plymouth for religious, economic, and political reasons.

subject: Social Studies
description: arrival of women and families
description_plain: arrival of women and families

subject: Social Studies
description: introduction of Africans as slave labor
description_plain: Your child understands how Africans were first brought to the colonies to work as slaves.

subject: Social Studies
description: private ownership of land
description_plain: private ownership of land

subject: Social Studies
description: representative government established through the House of Burgesses
description_plain: Your child will learn about the start of representative government through the House of Burgesses.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the English commitment to the permanent settlement at Jamestown as evidenced through the events of 1619 including:
description_plain: Your child can explain how the English showed they wanted to stay in Jamestown by events that happened in 1619.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the early successes and challenges of the Jamestown settlement including the leadership of John Smith, interrelationships with American Indians, challenges of the Starving Times, and the export of natural resources for profit.
description_plain: Your child will learn about Jamestown’s early challenges and successes, including John Smith’s leadership, relations with American Indians, the Starving Times, and exporting resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the economic and political motivations of immigrants and indentured servants who came to Virginia.
description_plain: Your child can explain why immigrants and indentured servants came to Virginia for economic and political reasons.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the economic and political motivations for English settlements at Roanoke and Jamestown .
description_plain: Your child will examine why the English settled at Roanoke and Jamestown.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize reasons for European colonization of North America and the impact on the development of the American colonies.
description_plain: Your child can summarize why Europeans came to North America and how this affected the colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will examine and compare the Jamestown and Plymouth settlements as the foundations of American culture and society.
description_plain: Your child will study and compare the Jamestown and Plymouth settlements as the beginnings of American culture and society.

subject: Social Studies
description: engage in authentic inquiry to acquire, refine, and share knowledge through written presentations related to social studies.
description_plain: Your child can research social studies topics and share what they learn through writing.

subject: Social Studies
description: summarize and paraphrase, integrate evidence, and cite sources to create written products, research projects, and presentations for multiple purposes related to social studies content.
description_plain: Your child can summarize information, use evidence, and give credit to sources when writing reports and presentations about social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will apply effective communication skills by developing a variety of evidence-based written products designed for multiple purposes and tasks, in order to demonstrate their understandings of social studies concepts, ideas, and content.
description_plain: Your child uses good communication skills to write different kinds of papers that show what they understand about social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: apply critical reading and thinking skills to interpret, evaluate, and respond to a variety of complex texts from historical, ethnic, and global perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can read and think carefully about complex social studies texts from different cultures and viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: comprehend, evaluate, and synthesize textual sources to acquire and refine knowledge in the social studies.
description_plain: Your child can understand, judge, and combine information from social studies texts to learn more.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will engage in critical, active reading of grade-level appropriate primary and secondary sources related to key social studies concepts, including frequent analysis and interpretation of informational sources.
description_plain: Your child reads and analyzes social studies materials to understand important ideas and information.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyze the principles of economic systems and develop an understanding of the benefits of a market system in local, national, and global settings.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different economic systems work and why market systems are helpful locally and worldwide.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate an understanding of geographic concepts and develop mastery of geographic tools and ways of thinking in order to become geographically informed.
description_plain: Your child understands geography and uses tools like maps to learn about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: develop skills and practices which demonstrate an understanding that historical inquiry is based on the analysis and evaluation of evidence and its credibility.
description_plain: Your child learns how historians study and check evidence to understand the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will utilize interdisciplinary tools and master the basic concepts of the social studies in order to acquire and apply content understanding in all related fields of study.
description_plain: Your child uses skills from different subjects to understand social studies topics better.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate the ability to investigate problems taking into consideration multiple points of view represented in arguments, structure of an explanation, and other sources.
description_plain: Your child can look at problems from different viewpoints and understand how arguments are made.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate the capability for developing essential, compelling, and supporting questions that address authentic civic issues.
description_plain: Your child can create important questions about real civic issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will determine the kinds of sources that will be helpful in answering essential, compelling, and supporting questions addressing authentic civic issues.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find the best sources to answer important questions about civic topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate understanding of the processes and rules by which groups of people make decisions, govern themselves, and address public problems.
description_plain: Your child understands how groups make decisions, govern themselves, and solve public problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate an understanding of the important institutions of their society and the principles that these institutions are intended to reflect.
description_plain: Your child knows about important institutions in society and the ideas they represent.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate an understanding of the virtues that citizens should use when interacting with each other and the virtues that guide official government institutions.
description_plain: Your child understands the good qualities citizens and government officials should have when working together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will understand the principles of government, the benefits of democratic systems, and their responsibilities as citizens.
description_plain: Your child understands how government works, why democracy is good, and what it means to be a responsible citizen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze reasons for conflict and cooperation among and between groups, societies, nations, and regions.
description_plain: Your child can analyze why groups, nations, and regions sometimes fight or work together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze global interdependence which explains the outsourcing of technological and manufacturing jobs to developing regions.
description_plain: Your child will learn why some jobs are moved to other countries and how countries depend on each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe patterns of global economic interdependence and trade, including the concepts of balance of trade and supply and demand; compare measures of economic growth including Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and Gross National Product (GNP).
description_plain: Your child can describe how countries trade and depend on each other, including ideas like supply and demand and how we measure economic growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how cultural diffusion, both voluntary and forced, impacts societies of a region.
description_plain: Your child will understand how cultures spread and change when people share ideas or are forced to move.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define the concept of region and identify the major political, physical, cultural, and economic regions.
description_plain: Your child can define what a region is and identify different types like political, physical, cultural, and economic regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will compare common physical and human characteristics of regions which create identity or uniqueness and influence people's perceptions of the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child will compare physical and human features that make regions unique and affect how people see the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of citizens as responsible stewards of natural resources and the environment.
description_plain: Your child understands that citizens should take care of natural resources and the environment responsibly.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the role of ecotourism in creating environmental awareness of resources, climate, cultures, and wildlife.
description_plain: Your child can evaluate how ecotourism helps people learn about the environment, climate, cultures, and wildlife.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze environmental challenges of each region.
description_plain: Your child can explain the environmental problems that affect different regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of climate and natural disasters on human populations, including forced migration, scarcity of consumer goods, economic activities, and loss of life.
description_plain: Your child understands how climate and natural disasters affect people’s lives, including causing moves, shortages, changes in jobs, and loss of life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the effects of human modification on the natural environment through transformation caused by subsistence and commercial agriculture, industry, demand for energy, and urbanization.
description_plain: Your child can explain how farming, industry, energy use, and city growth change the natural environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the commercial agriculture and industrial regions that support human development.
description_plain: Your child can describe farming and industrial areas that help people live and grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will analyze the interactions of humans and their environment in the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and the environment affect each other in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the common characteristics of developed and developing countries, including the impact of education and technology; analyze data used by geographers such as literacy rate, life expectancy, per capita income, and infant mortality.
description_plain: Your child can tell the differences between developed and developing countries by looking at education, technology, and facts like literacy and life expectancy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe benefits and limitations of the traditional, market, and command economic systems, including how government policies affect economic activities and trade relationships.
description_plain: Your child can explain the good and bad points of different economic systems and how governments affect trade and business.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate how the three levels of economic activities (primary, secondary, tertiary) contribute to the development of a nation and region.
description_plain: Your child understands how farming, manufacturing, and services help a country and region grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain topics related to indigenous sovereignty.
description_plain: Your child can identify and explain issues about the rights and independence of indigenous peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the role of the citizen in the selection of government officials and lawmaking; compare individual liberties under different forms of government.
description_plain: Your child understands how citizens choose leaders and make laws, and how freedoms differ under various governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the systems of government, including representative governments (democracy, republic, constitutional monarchy) and authoritarian systems (dictatorship, absolute monarchy).
description_plain: Your child can compare different governments like democracies and dictatorships and explain how they work.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the push and pull factors of migration have affected settlement patterns and the human characteristics of places over time.
description_plain: Your child can describe why people move to new places and how this changes where and how people live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of geography on population distribution, growth, and change, applying geographic concepts of population density, the availability of resources.
description_plain: Your child understands how geography affects where people live, how populations grow, and how resources are used.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe cultural traits of language, ethnic heritage, religion, and traditions practiced among peoples.
description_plain: Your child can identify and describe cultural traits like language, heritage, religion, and traditions of different peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify on a political map the major countries and population centers of each region.
description_plain: Your child can find major countries and cities on a political map for each region.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will identify the characteristics, distribution, and demographic patterns of human populations and systems of the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child understands the characteristics and patterns of people and governments in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the relationship and summarize the impact of the distribution of major renewable and nonrenewable resources on each region.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the location of important renewable and nonrenewable resources affects each region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the predominant natural resources found in each region.
description_plain: Your child can describe the main natural resources found in different regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the processes and factors of latitude, elevation, Earth-Sun relationships, prevailing winds, and proximity to bodies of water influence climate.
description_plain: Your child understands how factors like latitude, elevation, winds, and nearby water affect climate.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use visual information to identify and describe on a physical map the landforms, bodies of water, climate, and vegetation zones that are important to each region.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to identify important landforms, water, climate, and plants in each region.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will analyze the physical systems of the major regions of the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child understands the physical features and systems of major regions in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and analyze the role of geographic factors on current events and issues.
description_plain: Your child can explain how geography affects current events and issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Integrate visual information and develop the skill of mental mapping of the political and physical features of Earth's surface in order to organize information about people, places, and environments.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and pictures to mentally picture and organize information about places and environments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Integrate visual information, draw conclusions, and make predictions from geographic data and analyze spatial distribution and patterns by interpreting that data as displayed on geographic tools.
description_plain: Your child can look at geographic data on maps and tools to find patterns and make predictions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how various map projections distort the surface of the earth; apply the concepts of scale, distance, direction, relative location, absolute location, and latitude and longitude.
description_plain: Your child understands how different map types change the earth’s surface and can use map skills like scale, distance, and coordinates.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply geographic information to support analysis from primary and secondary sources located in a variety of texts.
description_plain: Your child can use geographic information to help understand facts from books and other sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will analyze data from a geographic perspective using the skills and tools of geography.
description_plain: Your child can use geography skills and tools to study data about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: engage in authentic inquiry to acquire, refine, and share knowledge through written presentations related to social studies.
description_plain: Your child can research social studies topics and share what they learn through writing.

subject: Social Studies
description: summarize and paraphrase, integrate evidence, and cite sources to create written products, research projects, and presentations for multiple purposes related to social studies content.
description_plain: Your child can summarize information, use evidence, and give credit to sources when writing reports and presentations about social studies.

description: Students will apply effective communication skills by developing a variety of evidence-based written products designed for multiple purposes and tasks, in order to demonstrate their understandings of social studies concepts, ideas, and content.
description_plain: Your child uses good communication skills to write different kinds of papers that show what they understand about social studies.

description: apply critical reading and thinking skills to interpret, evaluate, and respond to a variety of complex texts from historical, ethnic, and global perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can read and think carefully about complex social studies texts from different cultures and viewpoints.

description_plain: Your child can understand, judge, and combine information from social studies texts to learn more.

description_plain: Your child reads and analyzes social studies materials to understand important ideas and information.

description_plain: Your child can explain how different economic systems work and why market systems are helpful locally and worldwide.

description: demonstrate an understanding of geographic concepts and develop mastery of geographic tools and ways of thinking in order to become geographically informed.
description_plain: Your child understands geography and uses tools like maps to learn about places.

description: develop skills and practices which demonstrate an understanding that historical inquiry is based on the analysis and evaluation of evidence and its credibility.
description_plain: Your child learns how historians study and check evidence to understand the past.

description_plain: Your child uses skills from different subjects to understand social studies topics better.

description: demonstrate the ability to investigate problems taking into consideration multiple points of view represented in arguments, structure of an explanation, and other sources.
description_plain: Your child can look at problems from different viewpoints and understand how arguments are made.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate the capability for developing essential, compelling, and supporting questions that address authentic civic issues.
description_plain: Your child can create important questions about real civic issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will determine the kinds of sources that will be helpful in answering essential, compelling, and supporting questions addressing authentic civic issues.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find the best sources to answer important questions about civic topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate understanding of the processes and rules by which groups of people make decisions, govern themselves, and address public problems.
description_plain: Your child understands how groups make decisions, govern themselves, and solve public problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate an understanding of the important institutions of their society and the principles that these institutions are intended to reflect.
description_plain: Your child knows about important institutions in society and the ideas they represent.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate an understanding of the virtues that citizens should use when interacting with each other and the virtues that guide official government institutions.
description_plain: Your child understands the good qualities citizens and government officials should have when working together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will understand the principles of government, the benefits of democratic systems, and their responsibilities as citizens.
description_plain: Your child understands how government works, why democracy is good, and what it means to be a responsible citizen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how political, economic, and cultural forces challenge contemporary political arrangements leading to the devolution of states (civil wars, terrorism, genocide, and ethnic separatism).
description_plain: Your child can describe how political, economic, and cultural problems can cause conflicts like civil wars and terrorism.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze reasons for conflict and cooperation among groups, societies, and countries, including the creation and involvement of supranational organizations.
description_plain: Your child can explain why groups and countries sometimes fight or work together, including the role of international organizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze global interdependence which explains the outsourcing of technological and manufacturing jobs to developing regions.
description_plain: Your child understands how countries depend on each other, like when jobs move to developing regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain patterns of global economic interdependence and world trade, focusing on the concepts of balance of trade, supply and demand; compare the economic measurements of productivity, Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and Gross National Product (GNP).
description_plain: Your child can explain world trade and economics using ideas like supply and demand, and compare measures like GDP and GNP.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain patterns of global interdependence and world trade, including the impact of changing technology on trade routes.
description_plain: Your child understands how technology changes trade routes and affects global trade and connections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how cultural diffusion, both voluntary and forced, impacts society.
description_plain: Your child can describe how cultures spread and change through both choice and force.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define the concept of region and explain how and why regions change over time through physical and human processes which operate to modify the Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child understands what a region is and how regions change over time because of natural and human activities.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will compare common physical and human characteristics of regions which create identity or uniqueness and influence people's perceptions of the Eastern Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child can compare physical and human features that make regions unique and affect how people see them in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of citizens as responsible stewards of natural resources and the environment.
description_plain: Your child understands how citizens can take care of natural resources and the environment responsibly.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the role of ecotourism in creating environmental awareness of resources, climate, cultures and wildlife.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ecotourism helps people learn about and protect resources, climate, cultures, and wildlife.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the effects of human modification of and adaptation to the natural environment through transformation caused by agriculture, the use of modern irrigation methods, industry, demand for energy, and urbanization.
description_plain: Your child can explain how farming, irrigation, industry, energy use, and city growth change and adapt the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the differences among subsistence, cash crop and commercial agriculture, including the impact on economic development.
description_plain: Your child understands the differences between farming for food, cash crops, and commercial farming and how they affect the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how climate change is affecting environments and human populations.
description_plain: Your child can explain how climate change is affecting the environment and people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of climate events, weather patterns and natural disasters on human populations and the environment, resulting in forced migrations, scarcity of consumer goods, economic activities, and loss of life.
description_plain: Your child can explain how weather events and natural disasters affect people and the environment, causing things like forced moves, shortages, changes in jobs, and loss of life.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will analyze the interactions of humans and their environment in the Eastern Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child will study how people and the environment interact in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between developed and developing regions using the Human Development Index; analyze data used by geographers, including literacy rate, life expectancy, infant mortality, and per capita income.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between rich and poor regions by looking at things like education, health, and income.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess the influence of economic development and distribution of wealth on society.
description_plain: Your child will understand how a country’s wealth and economy affect its society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of government policies in utilizing wealth from natural resources to finance development.
description_plain: Your child can explain how governments use money from natural resources to help their countries grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the advantages and disadvantages of traditional market and command economic systems.
description_plain: Your child will learn the good and bad points of traditional market and command economies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the role of the citizen in the selection of government officials and lawmaking; compare individual liberties under different forms of government.
description_plain: Your child understands how citizens choose leaders and make laws, and how freedoms differ under various governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the structures of representative governments and authoritarian systems.
description_plain: Your child can compare how representative governments and authoritarian systems are organized.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the distribution of resources and evaluate how the three levels of economic activities (primary, secondary, tertiary) contribute to the development of a country or region.
description_plain: Your child will describe how resources are shared and how farming, manufacturing, and services help a country develop.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe reasons for and analyze from multiple perspectives the challenges and benefits of migration on indigenous and immigrant populations.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people move and how migration affects both native and new populations from different viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate and summarize the impact of geography on population distribution, density, growth, change, settlement patterns, the availability of resources, and migration, including push and pull factors.
description_plain: Your child will understand how geography affects where people live, how many live there, and why they move.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the impact of a region's major religions, including geographic hearths, major beliefs, customs, and the significance of religion in contemporary societies; explain how religion can both unify or divide people.
description_plain: Your child can explain how major religions started, their beliefs and customs, and how religion can bring people together or cause division.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare common cultural traits, including language, ethnic heritage, social systems, and traditions.
description_plain: Your child will compare common cultural traits like language, heritage, social rules, and traditions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify on a political map the major countries and population centers of each region.
description_plain: Your child can find the main countries and big cities on a political map of each region.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will identify the characteristics, distribution and demographic patterns of human populations and systems of the Eastern Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child will learn about where people live, how populations are spread out, and human systems in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the competition for scarce resources can cause economic and political conflict and cooperation.
description_plain: Your child can explain how fighting or working together happens when people compete for limited resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the distribution of major renewable and nonrenewable resources of each region.
description_plain: Your child will describe where important renewable and nonrenewable resources are found in each region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify on a physical map the major landforms and bodies of water of each region.
